narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 53.4% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to ensure we admit students likely to succeed. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances—without considering gender or race. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your class, far below the middle-upper range typical for those who passed the bar; this stood out as the biggest push toward predicting failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustaining high performance. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which actually worked in your favor—it's stronger than the 3.24 average among bar passers, pulling the prediction toward success and softening the overall risk. Your upper-middle family income also helped, aligning well with the majority of successful students and nudging the model slightly toward expecting you to pass. Other elements, like your notably low LSAT score of 26 compared to the 37 average for passers and your bottom-decile Year 1 ranking, added to the concerns but weren't the top drivers here. Full-time enrollment plans match what most admitted students pursue, so that didn't sway things much. In essence, while your college grades and family background compare favorably to those who succeeded, your weaker law school deciles dominated the prediction, tipping it into failure territory despite those positives. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model highlighting risks based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking ways to improve your class standing through tutoring or study groups, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that speak to your resilience. You might also explore post-bac programs or other law schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger next cycle. Keep pushing—you've already shown academic capability in undergrad that the model noticed.
